rationale: The execution chain originates from WinRS (remote execution via WinRM) spawning cmd.exe (EV-001), which launches a PowerShell process with an encoded command (EV-002), which in turn spawns a child PowerShell process (A0) — indicating remote lateral movement or remote code execution. The decoded payload (DER-001) implements a structured input-splitting wrapper that reads a piped payload, splits it on a null-byte delimiter, dynamically creates a ScriptBlock from the first segment, and executes it — a classic Ansible/framework-style execution wrapper, but the pattern of receiving and executing arbitrary piped code via [ScriptBlock]::Create() is consistent with Ansible's raw execution module or an attacker-controlled remote execution framework. The child PowerShell process (A0) then spawns chcp.com (EV-009), drops a DLL and cmdline file to Temp (EV-010, EV-011), and invokes csc.exe to compile them (EV-012, EV-013), confirming in-memory or on-disk .NET assembly compilation — a well-known technique for payload staging. The lsass.exe process_access events (EV-004, EV-005, EV-007, EV-008) with access mask 0x1478 (PROCESS_VM_READ | PROCESS_QUERY_INFORMATION | PROCESS_DUP_HANDLE) targeting the PowerShell processes are consistent with SSPI/Kerberos authentication token queries but warrant scrutiny given the broader context. The combination of remote execution, encoded multi-stage PowerShell, dynamic ScriptBlock creation from piped input, and runtime .NET compilation strongly indicates malicious or at minimum highly suspicious attacker-controlled activity.
